analogy: Think of "Money and Banking" through the chapter's own opening images: National Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting In this chapter we will introduce the fundamental functioning of a simple economy. In section 2.1 we describe some primary ideas we shall work with. In section 2.2 we describe... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Money and Banking is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that national Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting National Income Accounting In this chapter we will introduce the fundamental functioning of a simple economy.

The text explains that in section 2.1 we describe some primary ideas we shall work with.

The text explains that in section 2.2 we describe how we can view the aggregate income of the entire economy going through the sectors of the economy in a circular way.

The text explains that the same section also deals with the three ways to calculate the national income; namely product method, expenditure method and income method.

The text explains that the last section 2.3 describes the various sub-categories of national income.

The text explains that it also defines different price indices like GDP deflator, Consumer Price Index, Wholesale Price Indices and discusses the problems associated with taking GDP of a country as an indicator of the aggregate welfare of the people of the country.

The text explains that 2.1 SOME BASIC CONCEPTS OF MACROECONOMICS One of the pioneers of the subject we call in economics today, Adam Smith, named his most influential work – An Enquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations.

The text explains that what generates the economic wealth of a nation?

The text explains that these are some of the central questions of economics.

The text explains that it is not that countries which are endowed with a bounty of natural wealth – minerals or forests or the most fertile lands – are naturally the richest countries.

The text explains that in fact the resource rich Africa and Latin America have some of the poorest countries in the world, whereas many prosperous countries have scarcely any natural wealth.

The text explains that there was a time when possession of natural resources was the most important consideration but even then the resource had to be transformed through a production process.

The text explains that the economic wealth, or well-being, of a country thus does not necessarily depend on the mere possession of resources; the point is how these resources are used in generating a flow of production and how, as a consequence, income and wealth are generated from that process.

The text explains that let us now dwell upon this flow of production.

The text explains that people combine their energies with natural and manmade environment within a certain social and technological structure to generate a flow of production.

The text explains that in our modern economic setting this flow of production arises out of production of commodities – goods and services by millions of enterprises large and small.

The text explains that as we have mentioned before, there may fundamentally be four kinds of contributions that can be made during the production of goods and services (a) contribution made by human labour, remuneration for which is called wage (b) contribution made by capital, remuneration for which is called interest (c) contribution made by entrepreneurship, remuneration of which is profit (d) contribution made by fixed natural resources (called ‘land’), remuneration for which is called rent.

The text explains that in order to arrive at the market prices, we have to add to the factor cost the total indirect taxes less total subsidies.

The text explains that when indirect taxes are imposed on goods and services, their prices go up.

The text explains that thus, NNP at factor cost ≡ National Income (NI ) ≡ NNP at market prices – (Indirect taxes – Subsidies) ≡ NNP at market prices – Net indirect taxes (Net indirect taxes ≡ Indirect taxes – Subsidies) The foreigners have a share in your domestic economy.

The text explains that nFIA: Net Factor Income from Abroad, D: Depreciation, ID: Indirect Taxes, Sub: Subsidies, UP: Undistributed Profits, NIH: Net Interest Payments by Households, CT: Corporate Taxes, TrH: Transfers recived by Households, PTP: Personal Tax Payments, NP: Non-Tax Payments.

The text explains that thus, factor cost is equal to market prices, minus net indirect taxes.

The text explains that in barter exchanges, goods (or services) are directly exchanged against each other.

The text explains that the value of Indirect taxes – Subsidies was Rs 150 crores and National Income was Rs 850 crores.

The text explains that gDP (1+2) 18796955 Appendix 2.1 Appendix 2.1 Appendix 2.1 Appendix 2.1 Appendix 2.1 Source: Annual Report, Reserve Bank of India, 2024–25 (Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy)

The text explains that in Crore) Table 2.6: Components of Gross Domestic Product (2011–12) Constant Prices Appendix 2.2 Appendix 2.2 Appendix 2.2 Appendix 2.2 Appendix 2.2 Source: Annual Report, Reserve Bank of India, 2024–25 (Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy) 1.

The text explains that has the price level risen between the base year and the year under consideration?

The text explains that write down some of the limitations of using GDP as an index of welfare of a country. ?

The text explains that 35 35 35 35 35 National Income Accounting S.No.

The text explains that private Final Consumption Expenditure (PFCE) 10619579 2.

The text explains that government Final Consumption Expenditure 1707730 (GFCE) 3.

The text explains that gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF) 6333084 4.

The text explains that valuables 270758 Investment (3+4+5) 6923070 6.

The text explains that imports of Goods and Services 4229390 Net Exports (6-7) 161292 8.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Macroeconomics, Chapter 2: Money and Banking

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2.1 SOME BASIC CONCEPTS OF MACROECONOMICS and 2.2 CIRCULAR FLOW OF INCOME AND METHODS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Money and Banking; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Money and Banking through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2.1 SOME BASIC CONCEPTS OF MACROECONOMICS and 2.2 CIRCULAR FLOW OF INCOME AND METHODS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
